publication/title: Relationships between variance in electroencephalography relative power and developmental status in infants with typical development and at risk for developmental disability: An observational study
publication/authors: Hooyman, A; Kayekjian, D; Xiao, R; Jiang, C; Vanderbilt, D; Smith, B
publication/tags:
* EEG
* Developmental Disability
* Infant Development
* Resting-State EEG
* Relative Power Variance
* Machine Learning
* Cross-Validation
dataset/provenance:
* **Data Source Name**:
  * Resting state relative power EEG data and Bayley Scales of Infant Development Scales (version 3) scores: figshare (https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.699494613).

* **Total Data Points**:
  * N=32 infants (11 at-risk, 21 typically developing).
  * EEG data after rejection:
    * AR visit 1: N=11
    * AR visit 3: N=9
    * TD visit 1: N=21
    * TD visit 3: N=13

* **Previous Use**:
  * No. Not enough information is available.
dataset/splits:
* Number of Splits: Leave-one-out cross-validation.
  * This is the only split strategy mentioned.

* Split Counts:
  * Leave-one-out cross-validation was performed on each machine learning model to predict at-risk status among 32 infants (11 at-risk) with a mean age of 90 days.

* Class Distribution:
  * Leave-one-out cross-validation: 11 at-risk, 21 typically developing (TD).
dataset/redundancy:
* **Redundancy Methodology**: The study employed leave-one-out cross-validation. This method involves using a single observation from the original sample as the validation data, and the remaining observations as the training data. This process is repeated such that each observation in the sample is used once as the validation data.

* **Independent Sets**: No. The study is described as exploratory, and the initial use of machine learning on the entire dataset is not considered double dipping. The dataset was generated for the purposes of selecting a machine learning/statistical model. Future data collections would use the same data analysis to further confirm and add to the existing dataset as an independent set.

* **Independence Enforcement**: Not enough information is available.

optimization/encoding: * **Data Encoding Method**: The study used resting-state EEG data, ranging from 14-82 seconds, which was pre-processed to reject eye movement artifacts using independent component analysis. The variance of relative power was calculated by taking the standard deviation of the peak power of the entire frequency band (0–30 Hz) across the channels. This variance was then used as a feature for the machine learning models. The study also looked at power variance in individual frequency bins and found that peak power is typically within the theta frequency. Additionally, an analysis looking at the alpha frequency was predictive of 3rd visit scores using 1st visit resting-state EEG. The data was split into different visits and groups: AR visit 1 = 11, AR visit 3 = 9, TD visit 1 = 21, TD visit 3 = 13.
optimization/parameters:
* **Parameter Count (p)**:
  * Not enough information is available.

* **Architecture Selection**:
  * The authors tested various machine learning algorithms including logistic regression, one of the simplest linear models, using conventional EEG metrics (power and relative power) as input features for the AR classification task. The authors did not specify the method used to select the model architecture.

optimization/fitting:
* **p > N: No**

  The study involved 32 infants (11 at-risk) with a mean age of 90 days. The models used included logistic regression and multivariate linear regression. These models typically have a parameter count (p) that is much smaller than the number of training samples (N=32). For instance, a logistic regression model with k features would have p = k + 1 parameters, which is unlikely to exceed 32.

* **Over-fitting Evidence: Not enough information is available**

model/interpretability:
* **Model Interpretability**: Transparent
  * The models used in this study are logistic regression and linear regression, which are inherently interpretable. These models provide clear insights into the relationship between the input features and the output predictions. The coefficients in these models indicate the strength and direction of the relationship between each feature and the outcome variable.

* **XAI Methods**: None
  * The study does not mention the use of any specific Explainable AI (XAI) methods. The interpretability of the models is derived from the nature of the regression models themselves, which do not require additional XAI techniques to understand their predictions.

evaluation/method:
* **Evaluation Strategy**: Leave-one-out cross-validation.
  * Leave-one-out cross-validation was performed on each machine learning model to predict at-risk status among 32 infants.
evaluation/measure:
**Performance Metrics:**

* **Logistic Regression Model for AR Classification:**
  * Accuracy: 75%
  * True Negative Rate: 86% (18/21)
  * True Positive Rate: 55% (6/11)
  * Chi-square: 7.64
  * p-value: < 0.01
  * Degrees of Freedom: 2
  * Odds Ratio: 1.18
  * R-squared: 0.217
  * F-statistic: 8.33

* **Linear Regression Models for Bayley Scores Prediction:**
  * Adjusted R-squared (Adj R²) for various Bayley scores:
    * Bayley raw fine motor: 0.59 (null), 0.64 (full)
    * Bayley raw gross motor: 0.61 (null), 0.63 (full)
    * Bayley raw receptive language: 0.11 (null), 0.13 (full)
    * Bayley raw expressive language: 0.28 (null), 0.27 (full)
    * Bayley raw cognitive: 0.65 (null), 0.71 (full)
    * Bayley total raw score: 0.69 (null), 0.74 (full)
    * Cognitive composite: 0.17 (null), 0.22 (full)
    * Cognitive percentile rank: 0.06 (null), 0.07 (full)
    * Language composite: 0.11 (null), 0.11 (full)
    * Language percentile composite: 0.00 (null), 0.03 (full)
    * Motor composite: 0.19 (null), 0.30 (full)
    * Motor percentile rank: 0.09 (null), 0.17 (full)
  * ANOVA p-values for various Bayley scores:
    * Bayley raw fine motor: 0.03
    * Bayley raw gross motor: 0.12
    * Bayley raw receptive language: 0.22
    * Bayley raw expressive language: 0.43
    * Bayley raw cognitive: 0.02
    * Bayley total raw score: 0.01
    * Cognitive composite: 0.1
    * Cognitive percentile rank: 0.25
    * Language composite: 0.32
    * Language percentile composite: 0.18
    * Motor composite: 0.02
    * Motor percentile rank: 0.06
  * F-statistics and degrees of freedom for specific comparisons:
    * 2-regressor model vs. baseline model: F = 15.61, df = 2, p < 0.001, adj R² = 0.58
    * 3-regressor model vs. 2-regressor model: F = 14.13, df = 3, p < 0.001, adj R² = 0.65
evaluation/comparison:
* **Comparison Performed**: Yes
  * **Baseline Models**:
    * Baseline statistical model (age in days and at-risk status only)
    * Baseline model (intercept only)
    * 2-regressor model (age and at-risk status only)
    * 3-regressor model (age, at-risk status, and variance of relative power)
  * **Machine Learning Algorithms**:
    * Logistic regression
    * Multivariate linear regression
evaluation/confidence:
* **Statistical Confidence**:
  * **CIs**: No
  * **p-values**: Yes - p < .05
    * The p-value is from the computation of an F-test between the baseline model and the more complex model to determine if the sum of squares for the more complex model is significantly different than the simpler model.
    * The p-value represents the probability that the more complex multivariable linear regression model, baseline plus variance of relative power, is significantly better than the baseline model alone.
